Housing Rental Policies and Rent Growth Trends in the Chicago Metropolitan Area

Author

YC Zhang

Published

March 10, 2026

1. Introduction

Housing affordability has become one of the most pressing urban policy challenges in American cities. In Chicago — the third-largest metropolitan area in the United States — renters face a dual pressure: nominal rents have risen sharply since 2015, while income growth has not kept pace for a large share of households. This mismatch produces cost burden, defined as spending more than 30% of gross household income on housing, a threshold long used by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) as an affordability benchmark.

This project uses two publicly available datasets to examine three research questions:

  1. Temporal dynamics: How have rents evolved across Chicago ZIP codes from 2015 to 2026, and how did macro shocks — the COVID-19 pandemic and its associated eviction moratorium — shape that trajectory?
  2. Spatial inequality: Which Cook County census tracts bear the highest rent burdens, and is geographic clustering visible?
  3. Policy relevance: Do observable rent trends and burden concentrations align with the reach of key Chicago housing policies?

2. Data

2.1 American Community Survey (ACS) — Census Tract Level

The primary cross-sectional dataset comes from the U.S. Census Bureau’s 2024 5-Year American Community Survey (ACS). Three tables are used:

ACS Table Variable Unit
B25064 Median gross rent Census tract
B19013 Median household income Census tract
B25070 Gross rent as % of household income (burden bins) Census tract

All three tables cover Cook County, Illinois, which contains Chicago and its inner suburbs. After merging and cleaning (dropping tracts with suppressed estimates), the final dataset contains 1,254 census tracts.

Code 
import pandas as pd

acs = pd.read_csv("data/derived-data/acs_clean.csv")
print(f"Tracts: {len(acs):,}")
acs[["tract", "county", "median_rent", "median_income",
     "rent_to_income", "pct_burdened"]].head(8)
Tracts: 1,254
tract county median_rent median_income rent_to_income pct_burdened
0 101.00 Cook County 1168.0 74145.0 18.9 48.9
1 102.01 Cook County 1378.0 63056.0 26.2 42.9
2 102.02 Cook County 1323.0 53220.0 29.8 59.5
3 103.00 Cook County 1431.0 73105.0 23.5 44.2
4 104.00 Cook County 1347.0 54083.0 29.9 51.4
5 105.01 Cook County 1195.0 48833.0 29.4 53.3
6 105.02 Cook County 1383.0 63125.0 26.3 54.2
7 105.03 Cook County 1273.0 32443.0 47.1 49.6

Key summary statistics:

Code 
summary = acs[["median_rent", "median_income", "rent_to_income", "pct_burdened"]].describe().T
summary.columns = ["Count","Mean","Std","Min","25%","50%","75%","Max"]
summary = summary.round(1)
summary.index = ["Median Monthly Rent ($)", "Median Household Income ($)",
                 "Rent-to-Income Ratio (%)", "% Renters Cost-Burdened"]
summary
Count Mean Std Min 25% 50% 75% Max
Median Monthly Rent ($) 1254.0 1494.9 467.7 347.0 1185.2 1390.5 1717.5 3500.0
Median Household Income ($) 1254.0 85674.5 40204.6 13585.0 57361.2 78687.5 104961.0 249531.0
Rent-to-Income Ratio (%) 1254.0 23.8 9.2 5.5 17.8 21.9 27.4 108.5
% Renters Cost-Burdened 1254.0 46.4 16.9 0.0 34.6 46.1 57.1 100.0

2.2 Zillow Observed Rent Index (ZORI) — ZIP Code Level

The longitudinal dataset is Zillow’s Observed Rent Index (ZORI), a smoothed, seasonally adjusted measure of typical asking rents for single-family homes and condominiums. The raw file contains monthly observations for thousands of U.S. ZIP codes; this analysis filters to the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Core-Based Statistical Area, yielding 206 ZIP codes with observations spanning January 2015 through January 2026.

Code 
zillow = pd.read_csv("data/derived-data/zillow_clean.csv")
zillow["date"] = pd.to_datetime(zillow["date"])
print(f"ZIP codes: {zillow['zip_code'].nunique()}  |  "
      f"Date range: {zillow['date'].min().date()} – {zillow['date'].max().date()}  |  "
      f"Rows: {len(zillow):,}")
zillow[["zip_code","city","county","date","zori"]].head(6)
ZIP codes: 206  |  Date range: 2015-01-31 – 2026-01-31  |  Rows: 9,745
zip_code city county date zori
0 46304 Chesterton Porter County 2026-01-31 1425.000000
1 46307 Crown Point Lake County 2024-01-31 1600.368423
2 46307 Crown Point Lake County 2024-02-29 1627.205847
3 46307 Crown Point Lake County 2024-03-31 1623.777285
4 46307 Crown Point Lake County 2024-04-30 1635.703866
5 46307 Crown Point Lake County 2024-05-31 1617.062327

3. Results

3.1 Temporal Rent Trends by ZIP Code

The figure below plots the monthly ZORI for a representative sample of 15 Chicago ZIP codes, grouped into low-, mid-, and high-rent tiers based on their time-average rent level. Two policy events are marked with vertical reference lines.

Code 
import altair as alt

df_ts = zillow[zillow["city"] == "Chicago"].copy()
df_ts["zip_code"] = df_ts["zip_code"].astype(str)

annual_avg = df_ts.groupby("zip_code")["zori"].mean().reset_index().sort_values("zori")
low  = annual_avg.head(5)["zip_code"].tolist()
n    = len(annual_avg)
mid  = annual_avg.iloc[n//2-2 : n//2+3]["zip_code"].tolist()
high = annual_avg.tail(5)["zip_code"].tolist()
selected = low + mid + high

plot_df = df_ts[df_ts["zip_code"].isin(selected)].copy()

def tier(z):
    if z in low:  return "Low-rent"
    if z in high: return "High-rent"
    return "Mid-rent"
plot_df["tier"] = plot_df["zip_code"].apply(tier)

policy_events = pd.DataFrame([
    {"date": pd.Timestamp("2020-03-01"), "event": "COVID-19 / Eviction Moratorium"},
    {"date": pd.Timestamp("2022-09-01"), "event": "Moratorium Ends"},
])

line = alt.Chart(plot_df).mark_line(opacity=0.8, strokeWidth=1.6).encode(
    x=alt.X("date:T", title="Date"),
    y=alt.Y("zori:Q", title="Monthly Rent Index (USD)",
            scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
    color=alt.Color("zip_code:N",
        scale=alt.Scale(scheme="tableau20"),
        legend=alt.Legend(title="ZIP Code", columns=2)),
    strokeDash=alt.StrokeDash("tier:N",
        scale=alt.Scale(domain=["Low-rent","Mid-rent","High-rent"],
                        range=[[4,2],[1,0],[1,0]]),
        legend=alt.Legend(title="Tier")),
    tooltip=["zip_code:N","tier:N",
             alt.Tooltip("date:T", title="Date"),
             alt.Tooltip("zori:Q", format="$,.0f", title="ZORI")]
)
rules = alt.Chart(policy_events).mark_rule(
    color="gray", strokeDash=[6,3], opacity=0.65
).encode(x="date:T")
labels = alt.Chart(policy_events).mark_text(
    angle=270, align="right", baseline="bottom",
    dy=-5, fontSize=10, color="gray"
).encode(x="date:T", text="event:N")

import os
from IPython.display import Image
os.makedirs("figures", exist_ok=True)

ts_chart = (line + rules + labels).properties(
    title=alt.TitleParams(
        "Chicago ZIP Code Rent Trends, 2015–2026",
        subtitle="Zillow ZORI · Low/Mid/High rent tiers · Policy events marked",
        fontSize=14
    ),
    width=750, height=380
)
ts_chart.save("figures/ts_chart.png")
Image("figures/ts_chart.png")

Key findings:

  • Sustained growth (2015–2019): Average ZORI across Chicago ZIP codes rose from $1,493 (Jan 2015) to $1,541 (2019 average) — a modest 3.2% gain over four years, reflecting broadly stable conditions.
  • COVID-19 disruption (2020–2021): Rents dipped slightly in 2020–2021 (avg $1,532 → $1,521), consistent with pandemic-era demand shocks (remote work, population movement). The eviction moratorium kept many tenants housed but suppressed market rents temporarily.
  • Post-moratorium surge (2022–2024): Once the moratorium ended (September 2022), rents accelerated sharply. The 2023 average reached $1,728 — a 12.1% increase over 2019. By 2024, the average hit $1,820, representing a +21.9% cumulative gain since 2015.
  • Tier divergence: High-rent ZIP codes (North Side, Near North) grew both faster in absolute terms and recovered more quickly post-COVID than low-rent ZIP codes on the South and West sides, widening the intra-city rent gap.

3.2 Spatial Distribution: Choropleth Maps

The two maps below show, at the census tract level, (left) median gross monthly rent and (right) the share of renters who are cost-burdened (paying >30% of income on rent).

Code 
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker

gdf  = gpd.read_file("data/raw-data/tiger_tract/tl_2024_17_tract.shp")
cook = gdf[gdf["COUNTYFP"] == "031"].copy()
cook["geo_id"] = "1400000US" + cook["GEOID"]
cook = cook.to_crs(epsg=3435)
cook = cook.merge(
    acs[["geo_id","median_rent","pct_burdened"]],
    on="geo_id", how="left"
)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 8))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#1a1a2e")

def choropleth(ax, col, title, cbar_label, cmap, fmt_fn):
    data   = cook.dropna(subset=[col])
    nodata = cook[cook[col].isna()]
    vmin, vmax = data[col].quantile(0.02), data[col].quantile(0.98)
    nodata.plot(ax=ax, color="#3a3a4a", linewidth=0)
    data.plot(ax=ax, column=col, cmap=cmap,
              vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax,
              linewidth=0.05, edgecolor="#888", legend=False)
    sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap,
                               norm=mcolors.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax))
    sm.set_array([])
    cb = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, fraction=0.03, pad=0.02, shrink=0.72)
    cb.set_label(cbar_label, color="white", fontsize=10)
    cb.ax.yaxis.set_tick_params(color="white")
    cb.outline.set_edgecolor("white")
    plt.setp(cb.ax.yaxis.get_ticklabels(), color="white")
    cb.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(fmt_fn))
    ax.set_facecolor("#1a1a2e")
    ax.set_title(title, color="white", fontsize=12, pad=8)
    ax.axis("off")

choropleth(axes[0], "median_rent",
           "Median Gross Rent (2024 ACS)",
           "Monthly Rent (USD)", "YlOrRd",
           lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}")

choropleth(axes[1], "pct_burdened",
           "Share of Cost-Burdened Renters (2024 ACS)",
           "% Cost-Burdened (>30%)", "RdPu",
           lambda x, _: f"{x:.0f}%")

fig.suptitle("Cook County Housing Affordability · Census Tract Level",
             color="white", fontsize=14, y=0.98)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("choropleth_preview.png", dpi=130,
            bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=fig.get_facecolor())
plt.show()

Key findings:

  • North-South rent gradient: The highest-rent tracts cluster in the Near North Side, Lincoln Park, and Lakeview neighborhoods. Median rents in these tracts frequently exceed $2,000/month. The South Side and parts of the West Side show substantially lower median rents — some tracts below $900/month.
  • Burden does not mirror rent: Cost burden is highest not in the most expensive tracts, but in low-to-moderate rent areas where incomes are also low. 83.4% of Cook County tracts have more than 30% of their renters cost-burdened; 40.7% exceed the 50% threshold — meaning in nearly half of all tracts, the majority of renters are spending more than 30% of income on housing.
  • Geographic clustering: Severe burden concentrates in a contiguous band of South and West Side tracts (Englewood, Garfield Park, Austin, Roseland), consistent with historical patterns of disinvestment and lower household incomes.

3.3 Affordability Distribution

Code 
df_rti = acs[acs["rent_to_income"] <= 100].copy()
df_rti["burden_group"] = pd.cut(
    df_rti["pct_burdened"],
    bins=[0, 30, 50, 70, 101],
    labels=["Low (<30%)", "Moderate (30–50%)", "High (50–70%)", "Severe (>70%)"]
)

hist = alt.Chart(df_rti).mark_bar(opacity=0.8).encode(
    x=alt.X("rent_to_income:Q", bin=alt.Bin(maxbins=48),
            title="Annual Rent-to-Income Ratio (%)"),
    y=alt.Y("count():Q", title="Number of Census Tracts"),
    color=alt.Color("burden_group:N",
        scale=alt.Scale(
            domain=["Low (<30%)","Moderate (30–50%)","High (50–70%)","Severe (>70%)"],
            range=["#4daf4a","#ff7f00","#e41a1c","#984ea3"]
        ),
        legend=alt.Legend(title="Cost-Burden Rate", orient="top-right")
    ),
    tooltip=["burden_group:N", alt.Tooltip("count():Q", title="Tracts")]
)
rule30 = alt.Chart(pd.DataFrame({"x":[30]})).mark_rule(
    color="white", strokeDash=[5,3], opacity=0.7
).encode(x="x:Q")
lbl30 = alt.Chart(pd.DataFrame({"x":[30],"t":["← HUD 30% threshold"]})).mark_text(
    align="left", dx=4, dy=-120, color="white", fontSize=10
).encode(x="x:Q", text="t:N")

# ECDF
df_ecdf = acs.dropna(subset=["pct_burdened"]).sort_values("pct_burdened").reset_index(drop=True)
df_ecdf["ecdf"] = (df_ecdf.index + 1) / len(df_ecdf) * 100

area = alt.Chart(df_ecdf).mark_area(
    line={"color":"#e45756","strokeWidth":2},
    color=alt.Gradient(
        gradient="linear",
        stops=[alt.GradientStop(color="#e4575650", offset=0),
               alt.GradientStop(color="#e4575600", offset=1)],
        x1=1, x2=1, y1=1, y2=0
    )
).encode(
    x=alt.X("pct_burdened:Q", title="% Renters Cost-Burdened"),
    y=alt.Y("ecdf:Q", title="Cumulative % of Tracts"),
    tooltip=[
        alt.Tooltip("pct_burdened:Q", format=".1f", title="% Burdened"),
        alt.Tooltip("ecdf:Q", format=".1f", title="Cumulative Tracts %")
    ]
)
ref = alt.Chart(pd.DataFrame({"y":[50]})).mark_rule(
    color="gray", strokeDash=[4,3], opacity=0.6
).encode(y="y:Q")
ref_lbl = alt.Chart(pd.DataFrame({"y":[50],"x":[95],"t":["Median tract"]})).mark_text(
    align="right", color="gray", fontSize=10
).encode(x="x:Q", y="y:Q", text="t:N")

chart_hist = (hist + rule30 + lbl30).properties(
    width=380, height=300,
    title=alt.TitleParams("Rent-to-Income Ratio Distribution",
                          subtitle="Colored by cost-burden rate", fontSize=13)
)
chart_ecdf = (area + ref + ref_lbl).properties(
    width=380, height=300,
    title=alt.TitleParams("Cumulative Distribution: Cost-Burden Rate",
                          subtitle="Share of tracts with ≤ X% of renters cost-burdened",
                          fontSize=13)
)

dist_chart = alt.hconcat(chart_hist, chart_ecdf).resolve_scale(color="independent")
dist_chart.save("figures/dist_chart.png")
Image("figures/dist_chart.png")

Key findings:

  • The RTI distribution is right-skewed, with most tracts clustered between 20–40%. The median RTI across Cook County tracts is 21.9% — technically below the 30% threshold at the median, but this aggregate masks severe heterogeneity.
  • The ECDF (right panel) shows that only about 17% of tracts have fewer than 30% of their renters cost-burdened. The curve rises steeply between 30–60%, indicating that most tracts fall in the “moderate to high” burden range.
  • Tracts colored Severe (>70% of renters cost-burdened) appear across the full RTI range, including some with relatively modest median rents — confirming that burden is driven by income inadequacy as much as by high rents.

4. Interactive Dashboard

An interactive Streamlit dashboard supplements the static analysis above with dynamic filtering by city, ZIP code, year range, and variable selection.

Dashboard URL: https://datavisualizationfinalproject-m7rnhvqfe9uemeai2vqwjt.streamlit.app/

Note: Streamlit apps may need to be “woken up” if they have not been accessed in the last 24 hours.

The app provides three tabs:

  • Rent Trends Over Time — multi-select ZIP code filter, year range slider, and optional policy event overlay
  • Choropleth Map — switchable variable (rent / RTI / burden rate) and color scheme
  • Affordability Distribution — interactive histogram, ECDF, and rent-vs-burden scatter plot

5. Policy Discussion

5.1 The Eviction Moratorium and Post-Pandemic Rent Acceleration

Chicago’s COVID-19 eviction moratorium (March 2020 – September 2022, with phased rollbacks) kept tens of thousands of tenants housed during the pandemic but had an unintended market effect: it compressed rental market activity and modestly suppressed ZORI during 2020–2021. Once the moratorium was fully lifted, a surge in landlord re-pricing drove the sharpest two-year rent increase in the dataset — +12.1% from 2019 to 2023. This acceleration is consistent with national evidence on “catch-up” pricing after moratorium expiration.

The post-moratorium surge was not uniform. High-rent North Side ZIP codes recovered faster and peaked higher, while low-rent South Side ZIP codes showed smaller absolute gains but larger proportional income-to-rent deterioration, because starting incomes were already low.

5.2 Geographic Concentration and the Limits of Market Solutions

The choropleth maps reveal a persistent spatial pattern: severe cost burden is geographically concentrated in a contiguous corridor of South and West Side neighborhoods. These are the same areas that have historically faced redlining, disinvestment, and limited access to subsidized housing. High burden in low-rent tracts indicates that rents, even when nominally affordable, outpace incomes — a dynamic that market-rate construction (typically built in high-rent areas) does not directly address.

5.3 Policy Implications

Observation Policy implication
Post-moratorium rent spike Strengthen tenant relocation assistance and just-cause eviction protections
Severe burden concentrated in South/West Target voucher distribution and affordable unit production to high-burden corridors
83% of tracts exceed HUD 30% threshold Expand income-based rental assistance eligibility; current programs are insufficient in scale
Intra-city tier divergence Anti-displacement zoning overlays in rapidly appreciating North Side tracts

Chicago’s existing Affordable Requirements Ordinance (ARO) mandates that large residential developments include a share of affordable units, but the income thresholds and enforcement mechanisms have been criticized as inadequate. The concentration of burden shown in this analysis supports advocates’ calls for a strengthened ARO with higher affordable shares and deeper income targeting.

6. Conclusion

This analysis documents three interconnected patterns in Chicago’s rental market:

  1. Rents rose 21.9% between 2015 and 2024, with the sharpest acceleration following the end of the COVID-19 eviction moratorium in late 2022.
  2. Geographic inequality is severe and spatially clustered: the highest-rent areas are on the North Side and downtown, but the highest burden rates are concentrated on the South and West sides, where lower incomes make even moderate rents unaffordable.
  3. Burden is the norm, not the exception: 83% of Cook County census tracts have more than 30% of their renters cost-burdened, and 41% exceed the 50% threshold. This signals a systemic affordability failure rather than an isolated problem.

Together, these findings suggest that Chicago’s housing affordability crisis requires policies that address both rent levels and income inadequacy — neither supply expansion alone nor income supports alone will suffice. Spatially targeted interventions in high-burden corridors, stronger tenant protections, and scaled-up rental assistance represent the most evidence-supported near-term policy directions.

References

  • U.S. Census Bureau. (2024). American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates, Tables B25064, B19013, B25070. Retrieved from https://data.census.gov
  • Zillow Research. (2024). Zillow Observed Rent Index (ZORI). Retrieved from https://www.zillow.com/research/data/
  • U.S. Census Bureau. (2024). TIGER/Line Shapefiles: Census Tracts, Illinois. Retrieved from https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html
  • City of Chicago. (2023). Affordable Requirements Ordinance. Retrieved from https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/doh/provdrs/developers/svcs/aro.html
  • National Low Income Housing Coalition. (2024). Out of Reach 2024.
